input.background: Exercises 18.17 and 18.18 considered a case-control study of baldness and heart attacks in men using self-assessments of baldness. The published study also had interviewers assess the extent of baldness of study subjects using a Hamilton baldness scale (Figure 18.13). Table 18.32 cross-tabulates the data according to these interviewer assessments.
input.question: Compare the classification of baldness by interviewers (Table 18.32) with the self-assessed information in Table 18.21. Which method of assessment is preferable? Speculate how misclassification of baldness could bias the results of the study.

output.answer: Data show that subjects may be more optimistic than interviewers about the amount of hair on their head. For example, very few men self-assessed themselves as severely bald compared with interviewer assessments. Interviewer assessments are preferable because they are more objective and are likely to be more consistent than self-assessments. How would misclassification of baldness bias results? This is difficult to predict. However, if the controls were more optimistic than cases about their hair situation, this would bias the results away from the null.

Baldness_Interviewer.description: Baldness classification assessed by interviewers using the Hamilton baldness scale.

Cases_Interviewer.description: Number of cases (individuals with myocardial infarction) classified by interviewers.

Controls_Interviewer.description: Number of controls (individuals without myocardial infarction) classified by interviewers.

Baldness_Self_Assessed.description: Baldness classification self-assessed by study subjects.

Cases_Self_Assessed.description: Number of cases (individuals with myocardial infarction) self-assessed.

Controls_Self_Assessed.description: Number of controls (individuals without myocardial infarction) self-assessed.

meta_info.caption: Baldness and myocardial infarction, interviewer assessments.
